ci-grade-result-practice.title: Model a grade result
ci-grade-result-practice.prompt: Build a `GradeResult` class with `is_passing()` and `summary()` methods.
ci-grade-result-practice.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

ci-grade-result-practice.starterCode:
from models.grade_result import GradeResult

result = GradeResult("Sam", 84)
print(result.summary())
print(result.is_passing())

sc-constructors-and-object-state-design.title: Check the design choice
sc-constructors-and-object-state-design.prompt: Which choice best matches the lesson goal for constructors and object state?
sc-constructors-and-object-state-design.hint: Choose the option that keeps object responsibilities clear.

help.concept: Use `__init__` to create valid starting state and methods to update one object safely.
help.hint_1: Look for the answer that reduces duplication without hiding behavior.
help.hint_2: A good OOP answer usually keeps behavior near the object that owns the data.

options.a: Put every class in main.py so imports are never needed.
options.b: Give each class or service a focused responsibility and import it where needed.
options.c: Avoid methods so every object exposes all of its data directly.
options.d: Copy the same logic into each subclass to avoid shared code.

try-constructors-and-object-state-sketch.title: Create an Item constructor
try-constructors-and-object-state-sketch.prompt: Complete `Item` so each item stores a `name` and `quantity`.
try-constructors-and-object-state-sketch.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

try-constructors-and-object-state-sketch.starterCode:
from models.item import Item

item = Item("notebook", 3)
print(f"{item.name}: {item.quantity}")

try-constructors-and-object-state-sketch.title: Update object state with a method
try-constructors-and-object-state-sketch.prompt: Complete `Counter.increment()` so calling it changes the object and returns the new count.
try-constructors-and-object-state-sketch.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

try-constructors-and-object-state-sketch.starterCode:
from models.counter import Counter

counter = Counter(2)
print(counter.increment())
print(counter.increment())

concept.title: Constructors and Object State: the idea
concept.bodyMarkdown: **Constructors and Object State** is not just syntax. In this course it is practiced inside small projects where one file owns one clear part of the program. Use `__init__` to create valid starting state and methods to update one object safely.

multifile-shape.title: Constructors and Object State: the file shape
multifile-shape.bodyMarkdown: The file layout matters. Models hold object state and behavior, services coordinate groups of objects, tests verify behavior, and `main.py` stays thin enough to read.

graded-habit.title: Constructors and Object State: what the grader checks
graded-habit.bodyMarkdown: The grader checks behavior from the running project. A strong answer keeps imports working, keeps all files present, and proves the object behavior through output or assertions.
